modules.title: Getting Started with Agentic AI Labs
modules.subtitle: Explore the Environment Smoke Test and Minimal Acting Agent

slides.name: hook-intro-agentic-ai
slides.script: Ever wonder how to test if your AI environment is ready or build a minimal acting agent? Let’s kick off with the first two labs in Agentic AI Labs. You’ll see how to validate your setup and create the simplest agent that takes actions. It’s all about setting a solid foundation!
slides.title: Kickstart Your Agentic AI Journey

slides.name: environment-smoke-test-overview
slides.script: Lab 1.1 is all about the Environment Smoke Test. It’s a quick check to ensure your AI environment and dependencies are correctly set up. Think of it as a quick health check before diving deeper into agent building.
slides.title: Lab 1.1: Environment Smoke Test

slides.name: explanation-environment-smoke-test
slides.script: Let's dig deeper into why the environment smoke test matters and what each part validates before you run any agent code.
slides.title: Understanding the Smoke Test

slides.name: environment-smoke-test-code
slides.script: Here’s a snippet from Lab 1.1 that runs a simple environment check. Notice how it tries to initialize and perform a basic action. If this passes, you’re good to go!
slides.title: Smoke Test Code Snippet
slides.code:
def smoke_test():
    env = initialize_env()
    assert env.is_ready(), "Environment not ready"
    print('Smoke test passed!')

slides.name: code-flow-environment-smoke-test
slides.script: Let's walk through the smoke test flow. First, we call initialize_env to set up the environment. Then we assert it's ready—if not, execution stops immediately with a clear error. Finally, we print success. This three-step flow ensures you never proceed with a broken setup.
slides.title: Smoke Test Code Flow
points: Step 2: Assert readiness—fail fast if not
slides.code:
def smoke_test():
    env = initialize_env()
    assert env.is_ready(), "Environment not ready"
    print('Smoke test passed!')

slides.name: minimal-acting-agent-intro
slides.script: Moving on to Lab 1.2, the Minimal Acting Agent. This lab teaches you how to build an agent that performs the simplest possible action loop. It helps grasp the core mechanics of agents without any complexity.
slides.title: Lab 1.2: Minimal Acting Agent

slides.name: explanation-minimal-acting-agent
slides.script: Let's explore the minimal acting agent concept in more detail—how the action loop works and why starting simple sets you up for success.
slides.title: The Minimal Agent Action Loop

slides.name: minimal-acting-agent-code
slides.script: Here’s a key snippet that shows the agent’s action loop. It just picks a constant action and executes it repeatedly. This simplicity helps us grasp how agents interact with environments.
slides.title: Minimal Agent Action Loop

slides.name: code-flow-minimal-acting-agent
slides.script: The minimal agent loop has three logical steps. One: pick an action—here we always choose do_nothing. Two: execute it with env.step. Three: check if we're done and break the loop. This perceive-act-check pattern is the core of every agent, no matter how complex.
slides.title: Minimal Agent Loop Flow

slides.name: why-start-simple
slides.script: Why start with these simple labs? Because before building complex AI agents, you need solid basics. These labs prevent wasting time debugging environment glitches or convoluted agent logic. It’s like learning to walk before you run.
slides.title: Why These Labs Matter

slides.name: big-takeaway-agentic-ai-start
slides.script: The big takeaway? Always verify your AI environment first, then build a minimal agent to understand the action loop. These first steps give you a rock-solid base for all the advanced labs ahead.
slides.title: The Big Takeaway

modules.title: Exploring Agentic AI Labs 2.1 and 3.1: Intermediate Agent Concepts
modules.subtitle: Understand agent states, observations, and simple decision making

slides.name: hook-intermediate-agent-concepts
slides.script: Ready to level up? Labs 2.1 and 3.1 introduce key agent concepts like states, observations, and even simple decision making. We’ll break down what these mean and how the code brings them to life.
slides.title: Level Up Your Agent Skills

slides.name: lab-2-1-overview
slides.script: Lab 2.1 focuses on the agent’s internal state and how it updates based on observations. This is crucial because agents need memory or context to make smarter decisions rather than blind actions.
slides.title: Lab 2.1: Agent State Updates

slides.name: lab-2-1-code
slides.script: Here’s a snippet from Lab 2.1 showing how the agent updates its state from observations. Notice the state dictionary that accumulates info over time.
slides.title: Agent State Update Code
slides.code:
def update_state(state, observation):
    state['last_obs'] = observation
    state['steps'] += 1
    return state

slides.name: code-flow-lab-2-1
slides.script: The state update flow is simple but powerful. We receive the current observation and the existing state. First we store the observation as last_obs so the agent remembers what it just saw. Then we increment the step counter to track progress. Finally we return the updated state. This accumulation of context is what enables smarter decisions later.
slides.title: Lab 2.1 Code Flow
points: Return updated state for next decision
slides.code:
def update_state(state, observation):
    state['last_obs'] = observation
    state['steps'] += 1
    return state

slides.name: lab-3-1-overview
slides.script: Lab 3.1 introduces simple decision making. The agent now uses its state and observations to choose actions dynamically, not just fixed moves. This is a big step towards intelligent behavior.
slides.title: Lab 3.1: Basic Decision Making

slides.name: lab-3-1-code
slides.script: Here’s the decision logic snippet. The agent picks an action based on the current observation, like moving left if it sees an obstacle.
slides.title: Decision Making Code Snippet

slides.name: code-flow-lab-3-1
slides.script: The decision flow follows a simple if-else pattern. The agent receives state and observation. If the observation signals an obstacle, we return move_right to avoid it. Otherwise we continue forward. This conditional branching is the foundation of reactive behavior—the agent adapts its action to what it perceives.
slides.title: Lab 3.1 Code Flow

slides.name: why-intermediate-matters
slides.script: Why should you care about these concepts? Because understanding state and decision making lets you build agents that react intelligently, not just blindly. This foundation is vital for all future agent complexity.
slides.title: Why This Matters

slides.name: conclusion-intermediate-agent
slides.script: To wrap up, Labs 2.1 and 3.1 show how agents keep track of context and make simple decisions. Master these, and you’re well on your way to creating truly agentic AI.
slides.title: What This Means for You

modules.title: Mastering Advanced Agent Skills: Labs 4.1 to 6.1
modules.subtitle: State management, action policies, and environment interaction

slides.name: hook-advanced-agent-skills
slides.script: You’ve got the basics down, now let’s tackle Labs 4.1 through 6.1. We’ll dive into advanced state management, smarter action policies, and deeper environment interactions. Ready to see how agents get really powerful?
slides.title: Unlock Advanced Agent Skills

slides.name: lab-4-1-overview
slides.script: Lab 4.1 focuses on managing complex agent states, including nested data and timers. This helps agents handle tasks over multiple steps and keep track of progress or delays.
slides.title: Lab 4.1: Complex State Management

slides.name: lab-4-1-code
slides.script: See this snippet where the agent manages a countdown timer within its state, useful for delaying actions or waiting periods.
slides.title: State Timer Code Snippet

slides.name: code-flow-lab-4-1
slides.script: The timer flow uses lazy initialization. First we check if the timer key exists in state. If not, we initialize it to five. If it does exist, we decrement it by one. This pattern lets the agent track multi-step delays—for example, waiting a few steps before performing an action. The state survives across steps, so the countdown persists.
slides.title: Lab 4.1 Code Flow
points: Lazy init: set timer if absent

slides.name: lab-5-1-5-2-overview
slides.script: Labs 5.1 and 5.2 introduce action policies — rules that guide the agent’s choices based on state and observation. Think of policies as the agent’s strategy to decide what to do next.
slides.title: Labs 5.1 & 5.2: Action Policies

slides.name: lab-5-1-code
slides.script: Here’s a policy function that picks an action based on a simple heuristic — if the path is clear, move forward; otherwise, turn.
slides.title: Action Policy Code Example

slides.name: code-flow-lab-5-1
slides.script: The policy flow maps observations to actions. The function takes state and observation. If the observation indicates a clear path, we return move_forward. Otherwise we default to turn_right. This is the agent's strategy—a deterministic mapping from what it sees to what it does. The policy encapsulates the decision logic in one place.
slides.title: Lab 5.1 Code Flow

slides.name: lab-6-1-overview
slides.script: Lab 6.1 expands environment interaction by letting agents query environment info and adapt actions accordingly. This interaction loop makes agents more responsive and effective.
slides.title: Lab 6.1: Environment Interaction

slides.name: lab-6-1-code
slides.script: Check out this snippet where the agent queries environment sensors and adjusts its move based on detected obstacles.
slides.title: Environment Query Code

slides.name: code-flow-lab-6-1
slides.script: The environment interaction flow starts with a query. We call env.get_sensors to read the current state—here we care about the front sensor. If it's clear, we set action to move_forward. Otherwise we choose turn_left. The key is that the agent actively queries the environment instead of passively receiving observations. This enables richer, adaptive behavior.
slides.title: Lab 6.1 Code Flow
points: Select action based on sensor value

slides.name: advanced-skills-importance
slides.script: Why get comfortable with these advanced skills? Because real-world agents deal with complex states and unpredictable environments. Mastering these labs means your agents can handle real challenges.
slides.title: Why These Skills Matter

slides.name: conclusion-advanced-agent-skills
slides.script: Labs 4.1 to 6.1 unlock your agent’s potential with smarter state handling, policies, and environment awareness. This is where your AI starts to feel truly agentic and effective.
slides.title: Where We Go From Here

modules.title: Agentic AI Labs 7.1 to 9.1: Learning and Planning
modules.subtitle: Implementing learning loops, planning strategies, and goal-directed behavior

slides.name: hook-learning-planning
slides.script: Now it’s time to bring in learning and planning! Labs 7.1 to 9.1 focus on letting agents learn from experience and plan their actions to reach goals. This is where AI really starts to shine.
slides.title: Teach Your Agent to Learn and Plan

slides.name: lab-7-1-overview
slides.script: Lab 7.1 introduces learning loops — agents update their knowledge or policies based on feedback from the environment. It’s like trial and error but automated.
slides.title: Lab 7.1: Learning Loops

slides.name: lab-7-1-code
slides.script: Here’s a learning snippet where the agent updates a simple value estimate based on a reward received after an action.
slides.title: Learning Update Code
slides.code: value_estimate += learning_rate * (reward - value_estimate)

slides.name: code-flow-lab-7-1
slides.script: The learning update flow implements temporal difference learning. We take the reward we just received and compare it to our current value estimate. The difference—reward minus value_estimate—is the prediction error. We scale it by learning_rate and add it to our estimate. Over time, the estimate converges toward the true expected reward. This single line is the heart of learning from experience.
slides.title: Lab 7.1 Code Flow
points: Compute prediction error: reward minus estimate
slides.code: value_estimate += learning_rate * (reward - value_estimate)

slides.name: lab-8-1-overview
slides.script: Lab 8.1 adds planning. Agents now simulate future steps internally to pick the best action sequence. Planning lets agents anticipate outcomes rather than just react.
slides.title: Lab 8.1: Planning Strategies

slides.name: lab-8-1-code
slides.script: This snippet shows a simple planning loop where the agent evaluates possible next actions and picks the highest-scoring one.
slides.title: Planning Loop Code
slides.code:
best_score = -float('inf')
best_action = None
for action in possible_actions:
    score = simulate(action)
    if score > best_score:
        best_score = score
        best_action = action

slides.name: code-flow-lab-8-1
slides.script: The planning flow evaluates each candidate action before committing. We initialize best_score to negative infinity and best_action to None. Then we loop over possible actions. For each action we simulate its outcome and get a score. If that score beats our best so far, we update best_score and best_action. After the loop, we have the action that maximizes simulated outcome. This look-ahead is what separates planning from reactive behavior.
slides.title: Lab 8.1 Code Flow
slides.code:
best_score = -float('inf')
best_action = None
for action in possible_actions:
    score = simulate(action)
    if score > best_score:
        best_score = score
        best_action = action

slides.name: lab-9-1-overview
slides.script: Lab 9.1 focuses on goal-directed behavior — agents set and pursue goals using both learned knowledge and planning. This combines everything learned so far into purposeful action.
slides.title: Lab 9.1: Goal-Directed Agents

slides.name: lab-9-1-code
slides.script: Here’s code that shows how an agent updates its goal and chooses actions to minimize distance to that goal.
slides.title: Goal Pursuit Code

slides.name: code-flow-lab-9-1
slides.script: The goal pursuit flow is driven by distance. If we're farther from the goal than the threshold, we ask the planner for an action that moves us toward the goal. If we're close enough, we idle. This creates purposeful behavior—the agent acts until it achieves the goal, then stops. The planner handles the how; this logic handles the when.
slides.title: Lab 9.1 Code Flow
points: If close: idle until next goal

slides.name: why-learn-plan-goal
slides.script: Why integrate learning, planning, and goal-setting? Because this lets agents move from reactive machines to intelligent beings that improve and act with purpose. It’s a game changer.
slides.title: Why it Changes the Game

slides.name: conclusion-learning-planning
slides.script: Wrapping up, Labs 7.1 to 9.1 empower your agent with learning, planning, and goals. Mastering these makes your AI truly agentic and ready for complex real-world tasks.
slides.title: The Power of Learning and Planning

modules.title: Final Labs and Capstone: Mastery of Agentic AI
modules.subtitle: Integrating everything with labs 10.1 to 10.3 and the Capstone project

slides.name: hook-final-labs-capstone
slides.script: You’ve come a long way! Now let’s conquer the final labs, 10.1 to 10.3, and the Capstone. These bring everything together—complex agents, multi-goal management, and real-world challenges. Let’s see how it all fits.
slides.title: Bringing It All Together

slides.name: lab-10-1-overview
slides.script: Lab 10.1 focuses on multi-goal agents—agents that juggle several objectives and prioritize them smartly. This is critical for real-world scenarios where agents can’t just chase one goal.
slides.title: Lab 10.1: Multi-Goal Agents

slides.name: lab-10-1-code
slides.script: Here’s how the agent ranks goals by priority and picks the highest priority goal to pursue next.
slides.title: Goal Prioritization Code
slides.code:
goals = [{'name': 'A', 'priority': 2}, {'name': 'B', 'priority': 5}]
goals.sort(key=lambda g: g['priority'], reverse=True)
current_goal = goals[0]

slides.name: code-flow-lab-10-1
slides.script: The goal prioritization flow handles multiple objectives. We have a list of goals, each with a name and priority. We sort by priority in descending order so the highest priority comes first. Then we set current_goal to the first item. The agent can now focus on the most important goal while still maintaining the full list. When priorities change, we re-sort and pick again.
slides.title: Lab 10.1 Code Flow
points: Maintain list of goals with priorities
Select top goal as current focus
slides.code:
goals = [{'name': 'A', 'priority': 2}, {'name': 'B', 'priority': 5}]
goals.sort(key=lambda g: g['priority'], reverse=True)
current_goal = goals[0]

slides.name: lab-10-2-10-3-overview
slides.script: Labs 10.2 and 10.3 add robustness and adaptability. Agents learn to recover from failures, adjust plans, and optimize goal achievement under constraints.
slides.title: Labs 10.2 & 10.3: Robustness and Adaptability

slides.name: lab-10-2-code
slides.script: This snippet shows the agent checking for failure and triggering a replanning process to adapt.
slides.title: Failure Recovery Code
slides.code:
if env.failed:
    plan = replanner.replan(state)

slides.name: code-flow-lab-10-2
slides.script: The failure recovery flow adds resilience. Each step we check if the environment signals failure. If it does, we call replanner.replan with the current state. The replanner produces a new plan that accounts for what went wrong. We replace the old plan with this new one. The agent adapts on the fly instead of blindly continuing a broken plan.
slides.title: Lab 10.2 Code Flow
slides.code:
if env.failed:
    plan = replanner.replan(state)

slides.name: capstone-overview
slides.script: The Capstone project ties everything together. You build an agent that integrates all learned skills, handles complex environments, and achieves multi-goal objectives. It’s your ultimate test and showcase.
slides.title: Capstone Project Overview

slides.name: capstone-code
slides.script: Here’s a glimpse of the Capstone’s main loop where the agent continuously updates state, selects goals, plans, acts, and learns.
slides.title: Capstone Main Loop Code
slides.code:
while True:
    state = agent.update_state(env.observe())
    goal = agent.select_goal(state)
    plan = agent.plan(goal)
    action = plan.next()
    env.step(action)
    agent.learn(env.feedback())

slides.name: code-flow-capstone
slides.script: The Capstone loop integrates everything we've learned. Step one: observe the environment and update state. Step two: select which goal to pursue. Step three: plan toward that goal. Step four: get the next action from the plan. Step five: execute it with env.step. Step six: learn from the feedback. This observe-select-plan-act-learn cycle repeats forever. Each skill from the labs—state, goals, planning, learning—feeds into this unified loop.
slides.title: Capstone Main Loop Flow
slides.code:
while True:
    state = agent.update_state(env.observe())
    goal = agent.select_goal(state)
    plan = agent.plan(goal)
    action = plan.next()
    env.step(action)
    agent.learn(env.feedback())

slides.name: final-thoughts
slides.script: Why finish with these labs and the Capstone? Because mastery means integration. Separately these skills are powerful; together they create agents ready for the toughest AI challenges.
slides.title: Why Mastery Matters

slides.name: final-big-takeaway
slides.script: The big takeaway? The Agentic AI Labs guide you step-by-step from environment checks to building complex, adaptable agents. Master them, and you’re ready to push AI boundaries in the real world.
slides.title: Your Agentic AI Journey Complete
